Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 5. The new upper round bound is round 10; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 10; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

The revised draft is still **not a complete solution**. It explicitly states that the central small-\(p\) lemma remains unproved, and the “Remaining open issues” section again says that the implication
\[
[0,1/3]\subseteq \mathcal G
\]
is still missing. Therefore the proposed classification
\[
[0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}
\]
is not fully proved.

The new coalescence section is also problematic: the local formula is essentially correct, but the proposed coalescence assertion (C) is **false**, even under the cap \(w_i<p\). I give an explicit counterexample below.

---

## LaTeX contract check

I compiled the supplied `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`.

- Compilation succeeds.
- The PDF has 8 pages, within the 12-page limit.
- The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The `fullpage` package is used and is permitted.
- No forbidden layout packages or manual margin changes appear.
- No line-spacing changes appear.
- No in-document font-size changes such as `\small`, `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize` appear.

So the LaTeX contract is satisfied. The answer fails for mathematical completeness.

---

## Literature check

The Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis reference is bibliographically consistent: *Optimizing stakes in simultaneous bets* appears in ALEA 20, pages 153–165, 2023, and concerns maximizing \(\Pr(S\ge t)\) over convex combinations of iid Bernoulli variables. Its abstract says the relevant conjecture is proved “for a range” of \(p,t\), not in full generality, so it does not by itself supply the missing small-\(p\) lemma. ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf?utm_source=openai))

The Frankl reference is also bibliographically consistent: *On Sperner families satisfying an additional condition* appears in Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series A, Volume 20, Issue 1, pages 1–11, January 1976. The abstract concerns Sperner families satisfying a union restriction, related in spirit to the draft’s intersection-family reformulation but not a direct proof of the weighted-threshold biased-measure inequality needed here. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/009731657690073X?utm_source=openai))

---

## Previous concerns: status

### Addressed

1. **The \(p=0\) edge issue in the intersection-theoretic paragraph** has been fixed: the revised text now says “for \(0<p\le1/3\)” before defining \(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\).

2. **The notation in the unit-gap converse** has been improved: the residual weights are now denoted \(u_i\), avoiding the earlier ambiguity.

3. **The closedness and unit-gap reductions** remain present and are essentially correct.

### Still unresolved

The central issue remains: Lemma `\ref{lem:missing}` is still unproved. This lemma is exactly the missing positive direction for all \(0\le p\le1/3\).

### New issue introduced

The new coalescence assertion (C) is false. The draft presents it as a plausible sufficient finite reduction, but an explicit four-weight example contradicts it.

---

## Mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The universal interpretation is reasonable and faithful: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite probability vector of nonnegative weights.

However, the introduction still says:

> “I have not closed that lemma…”

This alone prevents the document from being answer-ready.

---

### Coloring criterion and positive families

The coloring criterion remains correct. For rational \(p=a/b\), random coloring into \(b\) colors and selecting a fixed \(a\)-subset of colors correctly produces iid Bernoulli\((a/b)\) selection indicators.

The reciprocal cases \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\), are correctly derived.

The pair-counting lemma for \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\), still appears valid. The deletion procedure and final count are correct.

The \(p=1/2\) argument is also correct: if
\[
X=\sum_i w_i v_i,
\]
then for \(p=1/2\), \(X\stackrel d=1-X\), so
\[
\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2],
\]
and hence \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).

No fatal issue in these positive partial results.

---

### Multiplicative closure

Proposition `\ref{prop:closure}` is valid. Representing Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables as products of independent Bernoulli\((p)\) and Bernoulli\((q)\) variables works, and the conditional normalization argument is sound.

No issue.

---

### Closedness of \(\mathcal G\)

Proposition `\ref{prop:closed}` is correct.

For fixed finite weights, the set of subset sums is finite, so as \(r\to p\) from either side, the threshold family stabilizes except for the distinction between \(\{w(A)\ge p\}\) and \(\{w(A)>p\}\). The proof handles this correctly.

This proves \(\mathcal G\) is closed, but does not prove that a dense subset of \([0,1/3]\) lies in \(\mathcal G\). Therefore it is only a reduction.

---

### Negative direction

The negative direction remains correct.

For \(1/3<p<1/2\), the equal-weight \(m=3\) example requires at least two successes, giving
\[
3p^2(1-p)+p^3=3p^2-2p^3<p.
\]
For \(1/2<p<1\), the equal-weight \(m=2\) example gives probability \(p^2<p\).

This correctly excludes all \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\).

---

### Small-\(p\) lemma

This is the fatal gap. Lemma `\ref{lem:missing}` states:
\[
0\le p\le1/3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
It is not proved, and the paper explicitly says it remains open in the draft.

Thus the full problem is not solved.

---

### Unit-gap formulation

The unit-gap equivalence appears correct.

Forward direction: applying Lemma `\ref{lem:missing}` to
\[
(1,b_1,\dots,b_n)/(B+1)
\]
and conditioning on the first Bernoulli variable gives the displayed unit-gap inequality.

Converse direction: if all original weights are below \(p\), selecting a maximal pivot \(a\), writing residual weights as \(u_i=ab_i\), and using \(a(B+1)=1\) correctly recovers the pivot inequality, which then gives the original inequality by conditioning on the pivot.

No serious issue here. But it is only an equivalence, not a proof.

---

### Complement, pivot, convex-hull, partition-function, and intersection formulations

These reformulations remain essentially correct.

Minor note: the convex-separation proof would be cleaner if it explicitly stated the orientation and strictness of the separating hyperplane. But the argument is standard and repairable.

The partition-function conversion is correct:
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)
=(1-p)^m\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\left(\frac p{1-p}\right)^{|A|}.
\]

The \(r\)-wise-intersection paragraph now correctly excludes \(p=0\).

Again, these are reformulations only.

---

## New coalescence section

The local coalescence formula is essentially correct. For \(q=1-p\), merging two weights \(x\le y\) changes the strict upper-tail probability by
\[
pq\left(
\Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]
-
\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]
\right),
\]
with the displayed endpoint choices matching the strict inequality convention.

However, the proposed coalescence assertion (C) is false.

Take
\[
q=0.7,\qquad p=0.3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=w_4=\frac14.
\]
Here all weights satisfy \(w_i=0.25<p=0.3\), so this is even inside the “nontrivial cap” regime mentioned by the author.

For the original vector,
\[
\Phi_q(w)
=\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(4,q)\ge3]
=4q^3(1-q)+q^4.
\]
At \(q=0.7\), this equals
\[
4(0.7)^3(0.3)+(0.7)^4=0.6517.
\]

If any pair is coalesced, the new vector is
\[
\left(\frac12,\frac14,\frac14\right).
\]
The event
\[
\frac12\eta_0+\frac14\eta_1+\frac14\eta_2>0.7
\]
occurs exactly when the merged coordinate succeeds and at least one of the two remaining coordinates succeeds. Its probability is
\[
q\bigl(1-(1-q)^2\bigr)
=0.7(1-0.3^2)=0.637.
\]
Thus every possible coalescence strictly decreases \(\Phi_q\):
\[
0.637-0.6517=-0.0147<0.
\]
So assertion (C), as stated, fails.

This does not make the main theorem false; it only invalidates the proposed coalescence route and the claim that computations found no capped counterexample.

There is also a small endpoint inaccuracy: the text says the one-coordinate vector has \(\Phi_q=q\). This is true for \(q<1\), but not for \(q=1\), where the strict event \(\eta>1\) has probability \(0\). Since \(p=0\) is trivial, this is minor, but the statement should be qualified.

---

## Final assessment

The revision improves some wording and adds reductions, but it still does not prove the required classification. The central small-\(p\) lemma remains unproved. Moreover, the new coalescence assertion is not merely unproved; it is false.

Therefore the draft is not answer-ready.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I would first modify the coalescence plan: the pairwise assertion (C) is false as stated.

Take
\[
q=\frac{7}{10},\qquad p=\frac{3}{10},\qquad m=14,\qquad w_1=\cdots=w_{14}=\frac1{14}.
\]
By symmetry every pair is the same. For a chosen pair \(x=y=1/14\), the remaining contribution is
\[
Z=\frac1{14}\operatorname{Bin}(12,7/10).
\]
In the coalescence formula,
\[
(q-x-y,q-y]=(0.5571\ldots,0.6285\ldots]
\]
contains exactly \(Z=8/14\), while
\[
(q-x,q]=(0.6285\ldots,0.7]
\]
contains exactly \(Z=9/14\). Hence
\[
\Delta=pq\left(\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(12,.7)=8]
-\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(12,.7)=9]\right).
\]
But
\[
\frac{\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(12,.7)=8]}
{\Pr[\operatorname{Bin}(12,.7)=9]}
=
\frac{\binom{12}{8}}{\binom{12}{9}}\frac{.3}{.7}
=
\frac{297}{308}<1,
\]
so \(\Delta<0\). There is no endpoint ambiguity here. Thus every possible pair-coalescence decreases \(\Phi_q\). The lemma itself is still fine in this example:
\[
\Phi_q=P[\operatorname{Bin}(14,.7)\ge10]\approx0.584<0.7.
\]

So I would not try to prove (C). A plausible salvage is **block coalescence** rather than pair coalescence. If a block \(B\) of total mass \(c\) is replaced by one coordinate of weight \(c\), and \(T_B=\sum_{i\in B}w_i\eta_i\), then the exact gain is
\[
\Delta_B
=
\mathbb E_Z\Big[
q\,\mathbf 1_{\{Z+c>q\}}
-\Pr(T_B>q-Z)
\Big].
\]
In the equal \(14\)-weight example, merging two loses, but merging a block of four already goes in the correct direction. This suggests choosing \(B\) by a random-permutation “first block whose mass is near \(p\)” rule and proving the average block gain is nonnegative. That would still give an induction-to-one-coordinate proof, but with adaptive block sizes.

A useful diagnostic identity for pairs is also worth recording. Fix a coordinate \(i\) of weight \(x\), let \(W_i=\sum_{k\ne i}w_k\eta_k\), and set
\[
S_i=\{q-x<W_i\le q\}.
\]
For \(j\ne i\) with \(w_j\ge x\),
\[
\Delta_{ij}
=
\Pr(S_i)\big(\Pr(\eta_j=1\mid S_i)-q\big).
\]
So pair coalescence asks for a pivotal strip in which some heavier coordinate is overrepresented. In the equal counterexample, \(S_i\) is the layer “9 successes among 13”, whose conditional marginal is \(9/13<.7\), explaining the failure. A block version should ask for an overrepresented block rather than an overrepresented coordinate.

For a non-coalescence route, I would focus on the partition-function formulation (4). It looks close to a fractional matching / weighted Erdős matching statement for down-sets, with dictatorship extremal and \(\lambda\le1/2\). I am uncertain about a black-box theorem, but Frankl–Füredi-style fractional matching results seem more aligned with (4) than biased halfspace or \(k\)-wise-intersection theorems.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
Three angles, in order of perceived promise:

**(1) Coalescence gain as a second discrete difference of the survival function.** Rewrite
$$\Delta_{ij}/(pq) \;=\; F_Z(q-x-y) + F_Z(q) - F_Z(q-x) - F_Z(q-y),\qquad F_Z(t) := \Pr[Z_{ij} > t].$$
This is $\ge 0$ iff $\int_0^x [F'_Z(u+y+s) - F'_Z(u+s)]\,ds \ge 0$ with $u = q-x-y$, i.e. supermodularity of $F_Z$ at those four corners — equivalently, $F_Z$ "convex on average" over $[u, q]$. Pointwise convexity fails because $\mathbb E[Z_{ij}] = q(1{-}x{-}y) > u$, so the lower endpoint is below the mode and $F_Z$ is concave at $u$. But the integral can still be $\ge 0$.

**Concrete conjecture to test computationally:** the **most-balanced pair** (minimising $|\log(w_i/w_j)|$, breaking ties by smaller sum) always gives $\Delta \ge 0$. Heuristically the four corners then straddle the mode of $Z_{ij}$ roughly symmetrically, and one can hope to extract supermodularity from log-concavity of Bernoulli convolutions (Bender 1973; Pitman 1997). The pivot+dust extremal gives $\Delta\to 0$ for the dust–dust pair, which is consistent.

**(2) Two-parameter Bellman invariant.** Your (Piv($a$)) is a single inequality with $g = 1{-}W$. Try the augmented family
$$\Pr[Y\ge T] - \lambda\Pr[Y < T-g] \;\ge\; c\,\Pr[T-g \le Y < T],$$
for weights summing to $W$. Conditioning on a new weight $u_1$ gives the *exact* decomposition $J(Y) = q\,J(Y'; T, g) + p\,J(Y'; T-u_1, g)$, so the family is closed under conditioning *as long as Piv holds for both* $(W{-}u_1, T, g)$ *and* $(W{-}u_1, T{-}u_1, g)$. The threshold floats but $g$ doesn't. The base case (singleton $Y = v_1\cdot u_1$) pins down the largest $c$ that survives; check whether $c = \lambda$ or $c = 1$ closes the recursion on the full region $\{(W,T,g): 0 \le T\le W,\, 0\le g\le T\}$ for $p\le 1/3$. The arithmetic isn't long; the question is whether the resulting $c$ is admissible at the dictator extremal.

**(3) Dubins–Savage / static "bold play."** Your conjecture is the static one-shot version of: in *sub*-fair gambling, bold play (a single all-in bet) uniquely minimizes the probability of reaching a fortune. The sequential version is DS Ch. 5; the static analogue is exactly the hypotenuse $t = q$ in FMP. Their proof on neighboring rectangles uses convex combinations of "double-or-quits" — those should extend to the boundary by a limit/perturbation argument. Pelekis has an arXiv manuscript (look for "weighted Bernoulli" + Pelekis) that addresses the triangular gaps; worth contacting him directly.

**(4) Literature worth checking before more work.**
- Pelekis & Ramon, "A lower bound on the probability that a binomial random variable is exceeding its mean," *Stat. Prob. Letters* **105** (2015), 124–130 — the exact discrete (uniform-weight) analogue. Their proof is short and may transfer.
- Greenberg & Mohri, "Tight lower bound on the probability of a binomial exceeding its expectation," *Stat. Prob. Letters* **86** (2014) — same regime.
- Léon–Perron 2003 (already cited): their Hoeffding-style "dispersing pair" technique is structurally close to your coalescence.

**My best bet:** start with (1)'s "most balanced pair" conjecture — five minutes of computer search will tell you if it survives. If yes, the proof reduces to a clean log-concavity claim about $F_{Z_{ij}}$. (2) is the cleanest formal induction if (1) doesn't crystallise. (3) is highest payoff via known literature.

### Council member: gemini-31-pro (models/gemini/gemini-31-pro) ###
This is a fascinating reduction. The coalescence strategy is mathematically extremely natural and strikes at the heart of the problem. Here is how I would approach the proof, combining your coalescence formulation with an averaging strategy inspired by Boolean Fourier analysis and continuous density heuristics.

### 1. The Supermodularity / Copula Angle
Your coalescence gain $\Delta_{ij}$ exactly measures whether the threshold function $H(\eta) = \mathbf{1}_{\sum w_k \eta_k > q}$ is "supermodular on average" under the $q$-biased measure. 

Distributionally, merging weights $w_i$ and $w_j$ is identical to taking independent $\eta_i, \eta_j \sim \mathrm{Bern}(q)$ and forcing them to be completely positively correlated (i.e., replacing both with a single $\eta_{\mathrm{new}} \sim \mathrm{Bern}(q)$) while preserving their marginals. This operation increases the expectation of any function that has positive second-order interactive differences. Specifically, your gain identity can be written exactly as:
\[
\Delta_{ij} = pq \cdot \mathbb{E}[D_i D_j H(\eta)],
\]
where $D_i f(\eta) = f(\eta \mid \eta_i=1) - f(\eta \mid \eta_i=0)$.

### 2. Continuous Intuition: Why $\Delta_{ij} \ge 0$
To see why this gain should be robustly positive, consider a continuous approximation. Let $Z_{ij}$ be the contribution of the remaining weights, and assume it has a smooth density $f_Z$. The difference operator $D_i D_j H$ acts on the step function $H$, so its expectation approximates the negative second derivative of the CDF of $Z_{ij}$. 

By Taylor expansion, $\Delta_{ij} \approx -pq \cdot w_i w_j \cdot f_Z'(q)$. 
Where does $q$ sit relative to the distribution of $Z_{ij}$? 
The mean of $Z_{ij}$ is $\mu = q(1 - w_i - w_j)$. Since $q \ge 2/3$, $\mu \ll q$, meaning $q$ is situated firmly in the **right tail** of $Z_{ij}$. Because Poisson-binomial distributions (and their continuous limits) are strongly log-concave, their density is strictly decreasing in the right tail. Therefore, $f_Z'(q) < 0$, which implies $\Delta_{ij} > 0$. 

### 3. Defeating Lattice Effects via Averaging
You noted that arbitrary or prescribed pairs (e.g., the two smallest) can fail and decrease $\Phi_q$. In light of the continuous heuristic, these failures are purely discrete **lattice effects**. Because $Z_{ij}$ is discrete, the positive interval $(q-w_i-w_j, q-\max(w_i, w_j)]$ might happen to fall between the atoms of $Z_{ij}$, while the negative interval catches a large atom.

To rigorously bypass these local lattice traps, I strongly suggest abandoning a deterministic pair-selection rule. Instead, prove that a specific **weighted average** of the coalescence gains is non-negative:
\[
\Sigma = \sum_{i < j} w_i w_j \Delta_{ij} \ge 0.
\]
If $\Sigma \ge 0$, by the pigeonhole principle, there *must* exist some pair with $\Delta_{ij} \ge 0$.

### 4. Connection to Second-Order Russo-Margulis
Why this specific sum? In the Boolean Fourier / Hoeffding decomposition of $H$, $\Sigma$ extracts exactly the total weight of the degree-2 (and higher) interactive coefficients evaluated in the direction of the weight vector $w$.

\[
- \frac{d^2}{dt^2} \Pr\left(\sum w_k \eta_k > t\right) \Bigg|_{t=q}
\]
Because $q$ is to the right of the mean $E[S] = q$, the survival function $\Pr(S > t)$ is locally concave at $t=q$ (since the density is decreasing). This global concavity guarantees that the second derivative is negative, forcing $\Sigma \ge 0$.

# Round 8 compute report: small-\(p\) coalescence check

I read the current `answer.tex` and `research_notes.tex`, especially the new complement/coalescence section.  The main outcome is that the proposed pairwise coalescence assertion (C) is false, even under the nontrivial cap \(w_i\le p=1-q\).  The failure is structural, not a numerical accident.

## 1. Literature search

I did not find a published theorem that directly implies
\[
\mu_p\{x:\sum_i w_i x_i\ge p\sum_iw_i\}\ge p\qquad(p\le1/3)
\]
for arbitrary nonnegative weights.

Closest precise references checked:

- Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis, *Optimizing stakes in simultaneous bets*, ALEA 20 (2023), 153--165, DOI `10.30757/ALEA.v20-07`.  Saved as `papers/fokkink-meester-pelekis-2023-official.pdf`.
  They define
  \[
  \pi(p,t)=\sup_\gamma \Pr\Big[\sum_i c_i\beta_i\ge t\Big],
  \qquad \sum_i c_i=1,\quad \beta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p),
  \]
  and prove bold-play optimality in several regions.  The theorem most relevant to our complement form is Theorem 4.4:
  \[
  \frac{k}{k+1}<p\le \frac{k+1}{k+2}<t
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  \pi(p,t)=p.
  \]
  Thus, for our complement target \(\Phi_q(w)=\Pr_q(S>q)\le q\), it proves the desired bound whenever the actual strict threshold \(t_*=\min\{w(A):w(A)>q\}\) lies above the next reciprocal \((k+1)/(k+2)\).  It does **not** cover the needed near-hypotenuse strip
  \[
  q<t_*\le \frac{k+1}{k+2},\qquad \frac{k}{k+1}<q<\frac{k+1}{k+2}.
  \]
  The paper's shaded-region theorem leaves exactly this kind of hypotenuse-adjacent strip; the authors also explicitly note such gaps elsewhere in the parameter diagram.

- Fishburn--Frankl--Freed--Lagarias--Odlyzko, *Probabilities for Intersecting Systems and Random Subsets of Finite Sets*, SIAM J. Algebraic Discrete Methods 7 (1986), 73--79, DOI `10.1137/0607009`.
  This gives sharp product-measure bounds for intersecting families in several cases and is used in FMP.  It gives the \(t>1/2\), \(p\le1/2\) bold-play block, but not the \(q>1/2\), \(t\downarrow q\) complement region needed here.

- Liggett-related result: the MathOverflow thread `papers/mathoverflow-278117-round8.html` records the known theorem that for \(p\ge1/2\), a convex combination \(S\) of iid Bernoulli(\(p\)) variables satisfies \(\Pr(S\ge1/2)\ge p\).  This is a fixed \(1/2\)-threshold theorem, not a through-the-mean theorem at threshold \(p\), so it does not imply the small-\(p\) lemma.

- Kellerer: I rechecked Kellerer’s *Linearkombinationen zufälliger Größen und ihre gemeinsame Verteilung*, Math. Z. 84 (1964), 403--414; EUDML metadata saved as `papers/kellerer-1964-eudml-round8.html`, existing PDF/images are in `papers/kellerer-1964-linearkombinationen-pages403-414.pdf` and `papers/kellerer_1964_pages/`.  I did not find a statement that specializes to the sharp Bernoulli halfspace inequality.  Kellerer’s “analogous regions” paper still looks related only at the convex-measure-embedding level, not as a black-box proof.

- Eaton/Hoeffding/Samuels/Feige: I downloaded Eaton’s technical report as `papers/eaton-1972-bounded-linear-combinations.pdf`.  Eaton and Hoeffding give upper-tail/normal-comparison inequalities for centered bounded sums; Samuels/Feige give small-deviation constants for sums of nonnegative variables.  These are not sharp enough for the equality regime here and do not give \(\Pr(S\ge\mathbb E S)\ge p\) with the exact constant \(p\).

## 2. Coalescence assertion is false

Recall \(p=1-q\), \(\Phi_q(w)=\Pr_q[\sum_iw_i\eta_i>q]\).  In failure variables \(F\), where each coordinate fails with probability \(p\),
\[
\Phi_q(w)=\Pr_p[w(F)<p].
\]
For a pair \(x\le y\), the exact coalescence gain is
\[
\Delta_{ij}=pq\left(
\Pr[p-x\le Z_{ij}<p]
-
\Pr[p-x-y\le Z_{ij}<p-y]
\right),
\]
where \(Z_{ij}\) is the \(p\)-biased failed weight of all other coordinates.  This endpoint convention matches the strict complement event \(w(F)<p\).

There is a clean infinite family of capped counterexamples.  Fix \(n\ge4\) and
\[
\frac1n<p<\frac1{n-1},\qquad q=1-p,\qquad w_1=\cdots=w_n=\frac1n.
\]
Then \(w_i=1/n<p\) and \(2/n>p\).  Hence \(w(F)<p\) iff \(F\) is empty or a singleton, so
\[
\Phi_q(w)=q^n+n p q^{n-1}.
\]
After coalescing any pair, the merged coordinate has weight \(2/n>p\), and only the \(n-2\) unmerged singleton failures are allowed:
\[
\Phi_q(w^{ij})=q^{n-1}+(n-2)pq^{n-2}.
\]
Therefore every pair has the same gain
\[
\Delta_{ij}
=\Phi_q(w^{ij})-\Phi_q(w)
=p q^{n-2}\bigl(n-2-(n-1)q\bigr)<0,
\]
because \(q> (n-2)/(n-1)\).  Thus no pair can be coalesced without decreasing \(\Phi_q\).

Concrete rational instance:
\[
p=\frac3{10},\quad q=\frac7{10},\quad
w=(1/4,1/4,1/4,1/4).
\]
Then
\[
\Phi_q(w)=q^4+4pq^3=\frac{6517}{10000}<\frac7{10},
\]
but for every pair
\[
\Phi_q(w^{ij})=q^3+2pq^2=\frac{637}{1000},
\qquad
\Delta_{ij}=-\frac{147}{10000}.
\]
So this is not a counterexample to the small-\(p\) lemma; it is a counterexample to the proposed coalescence proof route.

The requested averaged versions also fail on this example, since all six pair gains are equal and negative.  For \(w=(1/4)^4\), \(p=3/10\),
\[
\sum_{i<j}\Delta_{ij}=-\frac{441}{5000},\quad
\sum_{i<j}w_iw_j\Delta_{ij}=-\frac{441}{80000},
\]
\[
\sum_{i<j}(w_i+w_j)\Delta_{ij}=-\frac{441}{10000},\quad
\sum_{i<j}\min(w_i,w_j)\Delta_{ij}=-\frac{441}{20000}.
\]
Thus no nonnegative pair-weighting of the tested symmetric types can certify pair existence.

## 3. Computation files

New script:

- `code/round8_coalescence.py`

Outputs:

- `data/round8_coalescence_results.json`
- `data/round8_coalescence_summary.txt`

The script verifies the interval formula against direct \(\Phi_q\) computation, runs exact rational random searches, small exhaustive capped grids, and float minimax searches.  It also records the equal-weight trap family for \(n=4,\dots,9\).  Exact random search tested 8095 capped rational vectors and found the \(q=7/10\), \(n=4\) obstruction immediately; the parametric equal-weight proof above is stronger.

## 4. Suggested pivot

The coalescence route should not be used as stated.  A salvageable version would need a dichotomy:

1. either some pair has \(\Delta_{ij}\ge0\), allowing coalescence; or
2. the vector lies in a “reciprocal chamber trap” like the equal-weight examples above, where \(\Phi_q(w)\le q\) can be proved directly by a binomial tail.

The FMP/bold-play formulation remains the most promising literature route: it would be enough to prove the missing near-hypotenuse strip \(\pi(q,t)\le q\) for \(q\ge2/3\), \(t>q\), or only for the threshold values \(t=t_*(w)\) arising from weighted subset sums.

from scipy.optimize import linprog

def opt_quad(p):
    # variables a,b,c,t; h=a x^2+b x+c; E = a*(p*p+p*(1-p)*s2)+b*p+c >= t for s2 endpoints 0,p
    xs0=np.linspace(0,p,101)

    A=[]; B=[]

    for x in xs0:

    # h(x)<=1

    # maximize t -> minimize -t
    res=linprog([0,0,0,-1], A_ub=A, b_ub=B, bounds=[(None,None),(None,None),(None,None),(None,None)], method='highs')
    return res

notes_path='/mnt/data/file-RkJ3BGcq85NAXYiQZ2iGjc-research_notes.tex'
notes=Path(notes_path).read_text()
start=notes.index(r"\subsection*{Coalescence formulation for the complement problem}")
end=notes.index(r"\subsection*{Circle/interval representation}")
new_notes_coal = r"""\subsection*{Coalescence formulation for the complement problem}

For \(q=1-p\), the small-\(p\) lemma is equivalent to
\[
 \Phi_q(w):=\Pr_q\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q,\qquad q\ge2/3.
\]
If two weights \(x\le y\) are coalesced to \(x+y\), and \(Z\) is the sum of
the remaining coordinates, then conditioning on \(Z\) gives the exact change
\[
 \Delta
 =\Phi_q(\ldots,x+y,\ldots)-\Phi_q(\ldots,x,y,\ldots)
 =pq\left(\Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]-\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]\right).
\]
In failure variables with failure probability \(p\), the same formula is
\[
 \Delta=pq\left(\Pr[p-x\le Z<p]-\Pr[p-x-y\le Z<p-y]\right),
\]
with endpoint conventions adjusted to the strict event \(w(F)<p\).

The pairwise coalescence assertion ``some pair always has \(\Delta\ge0\)''
is false even when all weights are below \(p\).  Infinite equal-weight trap:
if \(n\ge4\), \(1/n<p<1/(n-1)\), \(q=1-p\), and
\(w_1=\cdots=w_n=1/n\), then \(w_i<p\) and
\[
 \Phi_q(w)=q^n+npq^{n-1}
\]
(empty or singleton failure set).  After coalescing any pair, the merged weight
\(2/n\) already exceeds \(p\), so allowed failure sets are empty or one of the
\(n-2\) unmerged singletons:
\[
 \Phi_q(w^{ij})=q^{n-1}+(n-2)pq^{n-2}.
\]
Thus
\[
 \Delta=pq^{n-2}\bigl(n-2-(n-1)q\bigr)<0.
\]
Concrete instance: \(p=.3,q=.7,w=(1/4)^4\), where
\(\Phi_q=0.6517\) and every pair-merge gives \(0.637\).

Possible salvage: prove a dichotomy saying either some pair has nonnegative
gain or the vector lies in a reciprocal-chamber configuration that can be
bounded directly by a binomial tail.  Block coalescence can also work where
pair coalescence fails: in the \(n=4,p=.3\) example, merging three of the four
weights gives a coordinate \(3/4>q\), hence value \(q\), above the original
\(0.6517\).  A useful exact block-gain formula: for a block \(B\) of total mass
\(c\), with internal random sum \(T_B\) and external sum \(Z\),
\[
 \Delta_B=\mathbb E_Z\left[
 q\,{\bf 1}_{\{Z+c>q\}}-\Pr(T_B>q-Z)
 \right].
\]
No general nonnegative proper-block theorem is proved.

\subsection*{Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis / bold-play formulation}

The complement inequality is a case of the FMP optimization problem
\[
 \pi(q,t)=\sup_{\sum c_i=1}\Pr_q\left[\sum_i c_i\beta_i\ge t\right].
\]
Our target is \(\Pr_q(\sum c_i\beta_i>q)\le q\).  For a fixed finite vector,
strict \(>q\) can be written as \(\ge t_*(c)\) where \(t_*(c)\) is the smallest
subset sum exceeding \(q\), so a theorem \(\pi(q,t)\le q\) for every \(t>q\)
would imply the small-\(p\) lemma.

FMP Theorem 4.4 proves bold play in rectangles
\[
 \frac{k}{k+1}<q\le \frac{k+1}{k+2}<t.
\]
This covers cases where the first attainable threshold above \(q\) jumps beyond
the next reciprocal \((k+1)/(k+2)\).  It leaves the near-hypotenuse strip
\[
 q<t\le \frac{k+1}{k+2},\qquad \frac{k}{k+1}<q<\frac{k+1}{k+2}.
\]
FMP Proposition 4.5 proves the boundary points
\(q=t=(k+1)/(k+2)\) for \(k>1\) (and identifies the exceptional
\(q=t\in[1/2,2/3]\) behavior).  For the original problem these boundary points
correspond to the reciprocal values \(p=1/(k+2)\).  The desired interval
\(p\le1/3\) is precisely the collection of upper-right hypotenuse strips
\(q\ge2/3\) that FMP note are suggested numerically but not fully settled in
their paper.

\subsection*{Bellman/unit-gap induction attempt}

The unit-gap inequality can be centered as follows.  For \(0\le b_i\le1\),
\(B=\sum b_i\), and \(\zeta_i\sim\Ber(p)\), put
\[
 S=\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p).
\]
Then (UG) is
\[
 \Pr[S\ge p]\ge \frac pq \Pr[S<-q].
\]
Thus one needs a two-tail comparison for a mean-zero sum whose positive jumps
are \(qb_i\), negative jumps are \(-pb_i\), and jump ranges are at most \(1\).

A tempting stronger induction would assert, for all shifts \(x\),
\[
 \Pr[S\ge x+p]\ge \frac pq\Pr[S<x-q].
\]
Conditioning on a coefficient \(b\) closes this family because it asks for the
same inequality for the residual sum at shifts \(x-qb\) and \(x+pb\).  However,
the zero-variable base case fails for \(x>q\).  The restricted domain
\(x\le q-pB\) is stable under conditioning but does not contain the required
point \(x=0\) when \(B>q/p\), which is exactly the hard large-dust regime.
This explains why a one-parameter induction fails.  A successful Bellman
argument likely needs an additional middle-interval term or a value function
depending on both the total remaining mass and the shift.

\subsection*{Influence/threshold-flow observation}

For a fixed weighted threshold down-set
\(D_t=\{A:w(A)<t\}\), define \(g(r)=\mu_r(D_t)\).  Russo's formula gives
\[
 -g'(r)=I_r(D_t),
\]
the total \(r\)-biased influence of the threshold.  The desired inequality is
\(g(t)\le1-t\) for \(t\le1/3\).  It would follow from
\(\int_0^t I_r(D_t)\,dr\ge t\).  The pointwise bound
\(I_r(D_t)\ge1\) is false for small \(r\) even when \(t\le1/3\): equal small
weights have very small initial influence.  Numerically, however,
\(I_t(D_t)\ge1\) appears to hold, with equality in dictator-type cases.  This
may be a useful endpoint/stability diagnostic but is not currently a proof.

"""

<council>
The remaining core problem is the unit-gap/two-tail inequality equivalent to the small-\(p\) lemma: for \(0<p\le 1/3\), \(q=1-p\), \(0\le b_i\le1\), \(B=\sum b_i\), and \(\zeta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)\ge p\right]
\ge {p\over q}\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)<-q\right].
\]
This resembles a martingale/ballot inequality for jumps \(+qb_i\) with probability \(p\) and \(-pb_i\) with probability \(q\), with all jump ranges at most \(1\). A naive shifted induction
\[
\Pr[S\ge x+p]\ge(p/q)\Pr[S<x-q]
\]
closes under conditioning but its base case fails for shifts \(x>q\); a restricted domain closes only when the remaining total \(B\le q/p\), not in the hard dust regime. Can you suggest a rigorous proof route for this two-tail inequality, or a precise known theorem (Kellerer/Kanter/Pinelis/ballot/size-bias/etc.) that implies it? If you think the inequality is false, give a concrete counterexample; otherwise prioritize proof strategies that could fit in a short LaTeX solution.
</council>
